1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	
7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140

300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}

456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).

505	  \[

515	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and

526	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is

590	  isomorphism}

594	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
595	  therefore extension of scalars along

599	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is

603	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for

618	      \;\longrightarrow\;
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
620	      \;\otimes\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
622	  \]
623	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).

626	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of

773	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be

776	  \[

778	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;

887	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
888	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)

910	\begin{lemma}
911	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
912	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
913	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.
914	
915	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
916	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
917	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
918	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
919	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
920	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
921	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
922	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
923	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
924	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
925	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
926	  left-whiskered morphism
927	  \[
928	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
929	  \]
930	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
931	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
932	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
933	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
934	
935	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
936	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
937	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
938	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
939	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
940	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
941	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
942	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
943	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
944	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
945	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
946	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
947	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
948	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
949	\end{lemma}
950	
951	\begin{proof}
952	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
953	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
954	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
955	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
956	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
957	
958	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
959	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
960	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
961	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
962	  \[
963	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
964	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
965	  \]
966	
967	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
968	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
969	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
970	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
971	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
972	
973	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
974	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
975	  linear maps (Mathlib
976	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
977	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
978	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
979	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
980	
981	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
982	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
983	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
984	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
985	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
986	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
987	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}

68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
96	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
97	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
98	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
99	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
100	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
101	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
102	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
103	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
104	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
105	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
106	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{lemma}\leanok
110	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
111	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
113	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
114	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
115	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
116	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
117	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
118	  isomorphism.
119	\end{lemma}
120	
121	\begin{proof}
122	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
123	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
124	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
125	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
126	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
127	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
128	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
129	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
130	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
131	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
132	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
133	\end{proof}
134	
135	\begin{lemma}\leanok
136	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
137	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
139	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
140	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
141	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
142	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
143	\end{lemma}
144	
145	\begin{proof}
146	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
147	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
148	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
149	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
150	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
151	  affine opens.
152	\end{proof}

154	\subsection{The affine computation}
155	
156	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
157	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
158	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
159	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
160	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
161	
162	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
163	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
164	their proof sketches.

166	\begin{lemma}
167	
168	\leanok
169	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
170	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}

172	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write

175	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
176	  \[

178	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
179	  \]

181	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying

188	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
189	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
190	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.

204	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules

229	\begin{lemma}

231	\leanok
232	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
233	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}

425	\begin{lemma}
426	% NOTE: The three route-iii supports now have their own pinned blocks above —
427	% `lem:powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core), `lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized`
428	% (section-level counit engine), and `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional` (the assembly
429	% that takes the per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis). The
430	% unconditional decl named in the `\lean{}` pin below is the one remaining open obligation: it must
431	% supply `hloc` for `N := (Spec φ)_* M^~` and feed it to the conditional form. The sole difficulty
432	% in `hloc` is the structure-sheaf smul carrier wall over `D(a)`, whose element-free resolution is
433	% the `D(a)`-specialization of `lem:gammaPushforwardIso` spelled out in the proof below. Pin stays
434	% on the unconditional decl until the prover closes it.
435	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
436	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
437	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
438	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
439	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
440	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
441	  \[
442	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
443	    \;\cong\;
444	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
445	  \]
446	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
447	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
448	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
449	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
450	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
451	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
452	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
453	
454	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
455	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
456	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
457	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
458	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
459	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
460	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
465	  lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
466	  lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
467	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
468	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
469	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
470	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
471	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
472	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
473	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
474	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
475	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
476	
477	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
478	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
479	  \[
480	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
481	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
482	  \]
483	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
484	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
485	
486	  \medskip
487	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
488	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
489	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
490	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
491	
492	  \medskip
493	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
494	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
495	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
496	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
497	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
498	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
499	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
500	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
501	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
502	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
503	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
504	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
505	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
506	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
507	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
508	  an isomorphism.
509	
510	  \medskip
511	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
512	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
513	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
514	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
515	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
516	  \(R[1/a]\); a naive section-level identity \(a \cdot x = \varphi(a) \cdot x\) does
517	  not present itself. The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
518	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
519	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
520	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
521	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
522	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). We construct \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the
523	  already-known \(R'\)-side localization, in element-free fashion, along a
524	  \(D(a)\)-level analogue of the \(\top\)-level identification
525	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}. This proceeds in three movements.
526	
527	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} In exact analogy to the
528	  \(\top\)-level Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, construct an \(R\)-linear
529	  isomorphism
530	  \[
531	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
532	      \;\cong\;
533	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) .
534	  \]
535	  Its construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim — two
536	  reconciliations of nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
537	  section maps, glued by a canonical isomorphism from the ring equation — except that the
538	  canonical isomorphism now rests on a \emph{\(D(a)\)-level} ring equation in place of
539	  the \(\top\)-level one of
540	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. The localization map
541	  \(R \to R[1/a]\) plays here the role that the global-section ring map plays at
542	  \(\top\).
543	
544	  \emph{(2) The \(D(a)\)-level ring equation.} The ring equation grounding this
545	  canonical isomorphism is the \(D(a)\)-shadow of
546	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}: writing
547	  \(g_R : R \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\) and
548	  \(g_{R'} : R' \to \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top)\) for the global-section
549	  ring maps and \(\mathrm{res}\) for the structure-sheaf restrictions,
550	  \[
551	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}
552	      \circ \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} \circ g_R
553	    \;=\;
554	    \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} \circ g_{R'} \circ \varphi ,
555	  \]
556	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
557	  definitional. This is the sheaf-naturality of the structure-sheaf comparison map
558	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) evaluated at \(D(a)\); it states that the
559	  \(\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top)\)-action on the pushforward sections over
560	  \(D(a)\) factors, after restriction, through
561	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{D(a)}\).
562	
563	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target
564	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) = M[1/\varphi a]\) is canonically the
565	  \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\)
566	  over \(R'\). Apply the ring-change converse
567	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
568	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
569	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) to
570	  obtain the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\) of the same module,
571	  viewed by restriction of scalars. Transporting that localization property back
572	  across the linear equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement (1) — via the
573	  transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized modules — yields
574	  the localization property at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\) on
575	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). Feeding the family
576	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
578	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\). The element-free \(\top\)-level template is
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, and the \(D(a)\)-level construction above is
580	  its specialization with the localization map \(R \to R[1/a]\) in place of the
581	  global ring map.
582	
583	  \medskip
584	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
585	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
586	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
587	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
588	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the substance of
589	  movement~(1): the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the structure-sheaf
591	  restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this open-naturality that drives
592	  the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the per-\(a\) localization fact
593	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level localization
594	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with the naturality
595	  square for the inclusion \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a
596	  section-level naturality square re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\).
597	
598	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
599	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
600	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
601	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
602	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
603	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
604	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
605	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
606	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
607	
608	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
609	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
610	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
611	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
612	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
613	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
614	  % bump.
615	  \medskip
616	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
617	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
618	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
619	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
620	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
621	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
622	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
623	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
624	  needed for the affine lane.
625	\end{proof}

627	\begin{remark}
628	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
629	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
630	  \begin{enumerate}
631	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
632	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
633	      \(R\)-module of sections of
634	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
635	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
636	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
637	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
638	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
639	      closed under isomorphism; hence
640	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
641	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
642	      needed for the affine lane.
643	  \end{enumerate}
644	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
645	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
646	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
647	  ingredient the affine lane requires.

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback is
2587	``extension of scalars'', and over a single ring extension of scalars is indeed
2588	strong monoidal. But the sheaf-of-modules pullback is defined \emph{abstractly} as
2589	a left adjoint: it has \emph{no} sectionwise value formula and \emph{no} stalkwise
2590	value formula, so one cannot literally attach a sectionwise \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2591	tensorator to \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\). Moreover the doctrinal transfer that Mathlib
2592	ships, \(\mathtt{Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), produces a \emph{lax}
2593	structure on the \emph{right} adjoint from an oplax structure on the left; the
2594	oplax comparison maps on the left adjoint itself are \emph{not} provided for free.
2595	Consequently the monoidality of \(f^*\) is established here by \emph{local-chart
2596	finality}, not by a global sectionwise tensorator: one exhibits the comparison maps
2597	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) and \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_Y\)
2598	and proves each is an isomorphism by restricting to affine charts on which the
2599	comparison functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) --- precisely the charts where the pullback
2600	\emph{is} locally extension of scalars --- and then globalizing ``iso on every chart
2601	\(\Rightarrow\) global iso''. This is the whole reason the general lemma is a separate
2602	result, with its own \emph{two}-phase proof (the cheap unit comparison and the
2603	genuinely new tensor comparison).
2604	
2605	It is worth noting why the monoidality route is the primary one rather than a
2606	detour. Restricting to \emph{flat} structure maps does \emph{not} help: flatness
2607	governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not whether the tensor comparison
2608	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) is an isomorphism (that comparison is an
2609	isomorphism for \emph{every} morphism, flat or not), so a flat restriction would
2610	simplify nothing here. The only genuinely simpler-looking alternative --- routing
2611	through local triviality, ``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\)
2612	\(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to locally \(\mathcal{O}\)'' --- is a mirage on this
2613	carrier: recovering \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse
2614	bridge \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly
2615	the dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
2616	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. The pullback-monoidality
2617	route is therefore primary precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse
2618	witness directly, never reintroducing the deleted dual.

2661	  comparison map must first be \emph{constructed} and then shown to be an isomorphism
2662	  by reduction to local charts. The argument has three parts: (a) build the
2663	  comparison map, (b) prove it is an isomorphism by a finality chart-chase, (c) observe
2664	  that this pointwise isomorphism is all the invertibility consumer needs (the full
2665	  strong-monoidal packaging is off the critical path).

2668	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) of \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso},
2669	  one constructs a tensor comparison
2670	  \[
2671	    \mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor} :
2672	      f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2673	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2674	      (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),

2677	  is not available off the shelf --- the Mathlib pullback ships only the composition
2678	  coherences \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\), \(\mathtt{pullbackId}\),
2679	  \(\mathtt{pullback\_assoc}\), and \emph{no} tensorator. It is produced from the
2680	  monoidal structure of the \emph{presheaf}-level pullback
2681	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) (where, unlike the sheaf level, the
2682	  ambient \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\) on presheaves of modules over a
2683	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaf \emph{does} exist), moved across the
2684	  sheafification using the genuine Mathlib lemma
2685	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- general (any morphism of
2686	  ring-sheaves) and already consumed in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- which
2687	  commutes \(f^*\) with sheafification. The reconciliation of the right-hand side
2688	  after this move is supplied by the already-established sheafification-monoidality
2689	  brick (``sheafification is monoidal up to the unit'', cf.
2690	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), which the eventual comparison consumes.
2691	
2692	  \emph{(b) It is an isomorphism, by local-chart finality.} This is the same
2693	  chart-chase that proves the unit comparison in \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, run one
2694	  level up. Cover \(Y\) by affine charts; on such a chart \(V \subseteq Y\) factor the
2695	  inclusion as \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) through
2696	  \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\), whose comparison functor
2697	  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,g.\mathtt{base}\) is \(\mathtt{Final}\) (via
2698	  \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\)). On a \(\mathtt{Final}\) chart the
2699	  pullback \emph{is} locally extension of scalars, which is \emph{strong monoidal} in
2700	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\): the local comparison is exactly the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2701	  tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S
2702	  (N \otimes_R S)\), an isomorphism for every ring map with no hypothesis. The
2703	  restricted global \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) is identified with this
2704	  local comparison through the naturality of the
2705	  \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\)/\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\)/\(\mathtt{pullbackCongr}\)
2706	  cluster --- the same glue wired in \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\). Finally
2707	  ``iso on every chart \(\Rightarrow\) global iso'' is the project's
2708	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}'s
2709	  globalizer). The residual work is the naturality lemma tying the restricted global
2710	  comparison to the local one.
2711	
2712	  \emph{(c) The pointwise isomorphism is all that is needed.} The sole consumer of
2713	  this lemma on the critical path, \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}, requires only the
2714	  comparison \emph{isomorphism at the single witness pair} \((M, N)\) together with its
2715	  naturality --- \emph{not} a strong-monoidal functor structure on \(f^*\). Parts
2716	  (a)--(b) already deliver exactly that: the comparison
2717	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) at \((M, N)\), shown to be an isomorphism by the
2718	  finality chart-chase. We therefore take the asserted isomorphism
2719	  \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\) to be \(\mathtt{asIso}\) of that
2720	  pointwise comparison, with naturality inherited from the naturality of the comparison
2721	  and of sheafification.
2722

2725	  \([\mathtt{IsIso}\,\eta]\) and \([\forall X Y,\ \mathtt{IsIso}\,(\delta_{X,Y})]\)) is

2729	  structure with all its coherence is not free. That packaging is \emph{not} needed for
2730	  the invertibility consumer and is deferred as off the critical path; the pointwise
2731	  isomorphism above is the deliverable.
2732	\end{proof}

2852	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2853	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2854	
2855	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2856	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2857	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2858	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2859	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2860	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2861	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2862	
2863	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2864	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2865	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2866	\[
2867	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2868	  \;:\equiv\;
2869	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2870	\]
2871	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2872	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2873	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2874	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2875	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2876	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2877	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2878	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2879	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).

2891	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2892	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2893	  \[
2894	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2895	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;

2898	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2899	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
